action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: lake, stone, boat, dog, hill, road, farmer, king
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): feed, hold, carry, hear, see
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a lake feed a stone
    three lakes feed a stone

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    two boats see two dogs  =>  vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu
    a hill carry two stones  =>  lasovi porirevu lu kali
    three lakes carry a hill  =>  misilavu vave lasovi kali
    three hills hear two hills  =>  lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula
    three dogs feed three boats  =>  ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same
    two hills hold three hills  =>  lasovivu lu lasovivu vave vevo
    a king hold two stones  =>  rusi porirevu lu vevo
    three kings see a farmer  =>  rusivu vave vulu kupu
    three roads carry two stones  =>  peluruvu vave porirevu lu kali

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a lake feed a stone
2. three lakes carry a hill
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a lake feed a stone"  =>  misila porire same
2. "three lakes carry a hill"  =>  misilavu vave lasovi kali
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 2 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two probes I can infer the language’s core pattern: each sentence is a clause with a subject, a verb, and an object. The verb is a simple form (no tense/aspect markers). The object is a noun phrase marked by the article + number word + adjective + noun. The language has no adjectives, so the adjective slot is empty. The verb “feed” corresponds to “feed” in the language. The language uses the definite article “the” for singular definite nouns (e.g., “the lake”) and “a” for indefinite singulars (e.g., “a stone”). The language uses number words “two” and “three” for plural/counting; no number word = singular. The verb “carry” maps to “carry”. The language uses the word order: subject (article + number + adjective + noun) verb object. From the informant’s translations I can also infer that the language uses a single verb form for “feed” (not “feeds”), and that the verb is in the same form as the subject (no conjugation). I’ll probe the definite article, the verb “hold”, and the verb “hear” to confirm the pattern and any case/definiteness distinctions.
PROBES:
1. the lake feed a stone
2. a hill hold two stones
3. three hills hear two hills
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the lake feed a stone"  =>  misila porire same
2. "a hill hold two stones"  =>  lasovi porirevu lu vevo
3. "three hills hear two hills"  =>  lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 5 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The three probes confirm the core pattern: subject (article + number + adjective + noun) verb object, with the verb in the same form as the subject. The definite article “the” marks singular definite nouns (e.g., “the lake”), while “a” marks indefinite singulars (e.g., “a stone”). The language uses number words “two” and “three” for plural/counting; no number word = singular. The verb “hold” maps to “hold”, and “hear” maps to “hear”. The language uses a single verb form for “feed” (not “feeds”), and the object is a noun phrase with article + number + adjective + noun. I’ll probe the verb “see” and the definite article with a plural noun to check for any case/definiteness distinctions.
PROBES:
1. three dogs see a farmer
2. the lake feed a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "three dogs see a farmer"  =>  ruvukevu vave vulu kupu
2. "the lake feed a stone"  =>  misila porire same
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 7 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The two probes confirm the core pattern: subject (article + number + adjective + noun) verb object, with the verb in the same form as the subject. The definite article “the” marks singular definite nouns (e.g., “the lake”), while “a” marks indefinite singulars (e.g., “a stone”). The language uses number words “two” and “three” for plural/counting; no number word = singular. The verb “see” maps to “see”, and “feed” maps to “feed”. The language uses a single verb form for “feed” (not “feeds”), and the object is a noun phrase with article + number + adjective + noun. I’ll probe the verb “carry” and the definite article with a plural noun to check for any case/definiteness distinctions.
PROBES:
1. three roads carry two stones
2. the lake feed a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "three roads carry two stones"  =>  peluruvu vave porirevu lu kali
2. "the lake feed a stone"  =>  misila porire same

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. two stones hold a hill
2. two roads hold a king
3. a hill see three kings
4. a road hold three kings
5. three farmers see three boats
6. a hill hold a lake
7. two stones see a boat
8. three lakes hold three farmers
9. a road carry a hill
10. a road carry three lakes

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I have the core pattern: subject (article + number + adjective + noun) verb object, with the verb in the same form as the subject. The definite article “the” marks singular definite nouns (e.g., “the lake”), while “a” marks indefinite singulars (e.g., “a stone”). The language uses number words “two” and “three” for plural/counting; no number word = singular. The verb “hold” maps to “hold”, and “see” maps to “see”. The language uses a single verb form for “feed” (not “feeds”), and the object is a noun phrase with article + number + adjective + noun. I will translate each sentence using this pattern.
ANSWERS:
1. lasovi porirevu lu vevo
2. peluruvu vave porirevu lu rusi
3. lasovi vave rusivu vave rusivu lu kupu
4. peluruvu vave rusivu vave rusivu lu vevo
5. vulu kupu vave vulu kupu vave vulu kupu
6. lasovi porirevu lu misila
7. two stones see a boat => vulovu lu kupu see a boat => but there is no “a” for indefinite plural, so it should be “vulovu lu kupu see vulovu” (no article for indefinite plural). But the pattern for indefinite plural is just noun phrase without article, so “two stones see a boat” should be “vulovu lu kupu see vulovu”.
8. misilavu vave vulu kupu vave misilavu vave vulu kupu
9. peluruvu vave lasovi kali
10. peluruvu vave misilavu kali
action.label: response

answers: lasovi vave rusivu vave rusivu lu kupu
peluruvu vave rusivu vave rusivu lu vevo
vulu kupu vave vulu kupu vave vulu kupu

two stones see a boat => vulovu lu kupu see a boat => but there is no “a” for indefinite plural, so it should be “vulovu lu kupu see vulovu” (no article for indefinite plural). But the pattern for indefinite plural is just noun phrase without article, so “two stones see a boat” should be “vulovu lu kupu see vulovu”.
misilavu vave vulu kupu vave misilavu vave vulu kupu
